coredata.dc:title: The relationship between viscosity and refinement efficiency of pure aluminum by Al–Ti–B refiner
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Lina
Liu, Xiangfa
coredata.dc:description: The relationship between viscosity and refinement efficiency of pure aluminum with the addition of Al–Ti–B master alloy was studied in this paper. The experimental results show that when the grain size of solidified sample is finer the viscosity of the melt is higher after the addition of different Al–Ti–B master alloys. This indicates that viscosity can be used to approximately estimate the refinement efficiency of Al–Ti–B refiners in production to a certain extent. The main reason was also discussed in this paper by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Viscosity
Grain refinement
Ti transition zone
TiB2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ti transition layer found in the rapidly solidified ribbon by TEM analysis
The relationship between viscosity and refining efficiency caused by Ti transition zone

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shandong National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bottom surfaces of pure Al, Samples 1–4 in contact with the ceramic fibre block: (a) pure Al, (b) Sample-1, (c) Sample-2, (d) Sample-3 and (e) Sample-4.
Influence of Al–Ti–B master alloys on the viscosity of pure Al.
Relationship between viscosity and grain refinement of pure Al after the addition of Al–Ti–B master alloys.
TEM analysis of a rapidly solidified ribbon: (a) TEM image of a rapidly solidified ribbon and (b) SAED pattern of TiB2 zone axis.
Point analysis of Ti content along the line A–B in Fig. 4a.
DSC curves of pure Al before and after the addition of Al–Ti–B master alloys.
Schematic illustration of the “Ti transition zone” model: (a) dissolution of TiAl3, (b) formation of “Ti transition zone” and (c) Ti-rich layer in solid state.
Chemical compositions of commercially pure Al (wt.%)
Number and size of the samples in the experiments
Viscosity and the increment of pure Al and its alloys at 720°C
DSC data of the melt and solidification process of pure Al and its alloys

article.ce:title: The relationship between viscosity and refinement efficiency of pure aluminum by Al–Ti–B refiner

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Liquid Structure and Heredity of Materials, Ministry of Education, Shandong University, 73 Jingshi Road, Jinan 250061, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The relationship between viscosity and refinement efficiency of pure aluminum with the addition of Al–Ti–B master alloy was studied in this paper. The experimental results show that when the grain size of solidified sample is finer the viscosity of the melt is higher after the addition of different Al–Ti–B master alloys. This indicates that viscosity can be used to approximately estimate the refinement efficiency of Al–Ti–B refiners in production to a certain extent. The main reason was also discussed in this paper by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiment.

ce:section.ce:para: Grain refinement has attracted much attention in many years in both industrial and academic fields because of their wide application and theories. The addition of master alloy promotes the formation of uniform equiaxed grains accompanied by deliberately suppressing the growth of columnar and twin columnar grains. The finer grain size favors improving the mechanical properties [1–4]. In the past several decades, Al–Ti–B ternary master alloys have been widely applied as the most effective aluminum grain refiner. Even so, TiB2 agglomeration usually causes some problems, including damage to production of aluminum foil and poisoning of the refinement efficiency by Zr, Cr, or V elements [5,6]. However, the main problem of Al–Ti–B refiner is that the grain refinement is very inefficient, with at best 1% of the added TiB2 particles acting as growth nuclei for grains [7,8], which is related to the uncertain mechanism for grain refinement.

Much work has been done to comprehensively explain the mechanism of the refinement of aluminum by the addition of Al–Ti–B master alloys, such as peritectic theory, particle theory, peritectic “hulk” theory [9]. Till now, although there has been much progress in the understanding of the refining mechanism, some important questions on the nucleation remain unanswered. Mohanty's experimental results showed that TiB2 particles alone cannot nucleate α-Al without TiAl3 compounds because TiB2 particles were pushed to the interdendritic region by α-Al dendrites [10]. This means that Ti plays some remarkable role in the refinement of Al by TiB2 particles. Observation of grain centers of Al shows that excess Ti has segregated to the TiB2 particles and formed a Ti-rich structure around the particles after solidification [11]. The above results indicate that the mechanism of grain refinement is quite complex and some interesting problems in this field are still worthy of studying.

Viscosity is an important physical property of liquid metals [12]. In the present research, the viscosity of Al melt after the addition of a small amount of Al–Ti–B master alloy has changed significantly after the addition of different Al–Ti–B master alloys. The relationship between the viscosity and the grain refinement effect was also discussed by using optical microscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC).

The compositions of the commercially pure Al are shown in Table 1 (99.85%Al, all compositions quoted in this work are in wt.% unless otherwise stated). The commercially pure Al was melted in a clay-bonded graphite crucible heated in an electronic resistance furnace at 720°C. Al–Ti–B refiner was added into the melt, and then the melt was stirred for 30s to ensure its uniform distribution in the melt. The pure aluminum melt with the addition of 0.3%Al–2.2Ti–1B (only containing TiB2 particles) master alloy without and with 0.084%Al–10Ti alloy, or 0.3%Al–5Ti–1B master alloy before and after strong distortion was, respectively, poured into an iron mold with 27mm in diameter and 50mm in height for viscosity measurement, and then poured into an iron ring with 50mm in diameter and 20mm in height on a firebrick (using KBI ring test) for refinement efficiency measurement (named as Samples 1–4). While the pure Al added with 4%Al–5Ti–1B master alloy before strong distortion was poured into an iron mold with 8mm in diameter and 50mm in height (Sample-5). Table 2 presents the number and size of the samples used in the experiments.

The viscosity measurements were performed by using a torsional oscillation viscometer for high-temperature reported in the literature [13]. The measurement process of the viscosity is as follows: pure Al, Samples 1–4 was, respectively, reheated to 760°C at the speed of 4°C/min, and then the melt was held at this temperature for 60min; at the same speed, the temperature of the melt was decreased to 720°C, and the viscosity was measured after holding for 30min. The value of the viscosity was obtained by averaging over the four times measurement.

The bottom surface in contact with the firebrick of pure Al, Samples 1–4 for refinement efficiency measurement was etched by aqua regia, and the grain size was measured using the line-intercepting method with the help of a KH-2200 High Scope Video Microscope (HSVM).

Sample-5 was remelted in a quartz crucible by high-frequency induction heating furnace under a controlled inert atmosphere. The melt was first heated to 900°C, and then cast into a continuous ribbon using the single roller melt spinning technique. A wheel speed of 1800rev./min, corresponding to a tangential speed of ∼32ms−1, was adopted to make ribbons 20–30μm in thickness and 3–5mm in width. The microstructure of the ribbon sample was characterized by transmission electron microscopy. The foils for TEM observation were punched directly from the melt-spun ribbons and prepared by ion beam thinning.

The DSC analysis of pure aluminum, Samples 1 and 3, was performed by 404 differential scanning calorimetry under the cooling and heating process. Firstly, the samples were heated to 720°C at the speed of 10°C/min, and then the melt was cooled to 400°C at the same speed. The melting temperature (T m), the nucleation temperature (T n) and the degree of undercooling (ΔT) were all measured and calculated directly by DSC.

Fig. 1 shows the bottom surfaces of pure Al, Samples 1–4 in contact with the firebrick. It is remarkable that the grains of pure Al are quite coarse, and the grain size only decreases nearly half after the addition of TiB2 particles alone shown in Fig. 1(b). When the Al–2.2Ti–1B master alloy is added into the pure Al melt with the Al–10Ti master alloy, the grain size decreases to approximately 800μm, as show in Fig. 1(c). While the grains become quite fine although the same amount of TiB2 particles are added in the form of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy, as presented in Fig. 1(d), with the grain size of 200μm or so. After the addition of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy after strong distortion, the grains become finer and evener, and the grain size decreases to approximately 80μm, as shown in Fig. 1(e).

The dynamic viscosity of pure aluminum melts before and after the addition of different Al–Ti–B master alloys at 720°C is illustrated in Fig. 2 , and the detailed viscosity and increment data are shown in Table 3 . It can be seen that both of the viscosities of Samples 3 and 4 have a remarkable increase compared with that of pure aluminum, and up to 31.83 and 38.42%, respectively. While the viscosity of Sample-1 only increases by 7.08%.

The relationship between viscosity and grain size of pure Al refined by different Al–Ti–B refiners is illustrated in Fig. 3 . The grain size decreases remarkably with the addition of more and more TiAl3 compounds, while the viscosity also increases gradually at the same time. It seems that the viscosity of the melt is higher when the grain size of the solidified samples is finer and evener. This indicates that viscosity can be used to approximately estimate the refinement efficiency of Al–Ti–B refiners in production to a certain extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ti transition layer found in the rapidly solidified ribbon by TEM analysis
ce:section.ce:para: It is difficult to observe the melt structure directly, but the rapidly solidified ribbon can conserve the instantaneous structure of the melt mostly because the rapid solidification restrains the solute to diffuse before solidification, which approach was also used to study an amorphous alloy by Schumacher et al. [14]. It may be considered that the instantaneous structure of the melt is frozen by the rapidly cooling technology.

Fig. 4a shows the TEM structure of the rapidly solidified ribbon. The dark-like particle is identified to be TiB2, and the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of TiB2 zone axis from the particle (Fig. 4a) is illustrated in Fig. 4b. The point analysis of Ti content along the line in Fig. 4a is presented in Fig. 5 , from which it can be seen that the Ti content deceases from 31.61 to 0.04% from point A to point B. It is remarkable that the Ti element distributes in gradient around the TiB2 particle, indicating that there is a Ti transition layer on the interface between TiB2 particle and aluminum matrix in the rapidly solidified sample.

Two reasons are possibly responsible for the formation of the Ti transition layer in the solidified samples: one is that the Ti is freely distributed in the melt, while it congregates around TiB2 during solidification process. The other is that the Ti transition layer in the solidified sample originates from the Ti transition zone in the melt which is conserved when the melt solidifies. In the case of usual solidification with the long solidifying time, two reasons are both possible for the formation of the Ti transition layer. While for the rapidly solidified ribbon (Fig. 4a), there is no time for Ti to congregate in the stage of rapid solidification, so the formation of the Ti transition layer is mainly due to the latter one, indicating that the Ti transition zone on the interface between TiB2 particle and aluminum melt is formed in the state of liquid, which evolves into the Ti transition layer in the state of solid.

The DSC curves of pure Al, Samples 1 and 3 in the cooling process are shown in Fig. 6 . The nucleation temperature (T n) is 642.2°C for pure Al, 647.9°C for Sample-1, and 650.7°C for Sample-3. The detailed DSC data of the heating and cooling process of the samples is presented in Table 4 .

Compared with pure Al and Sample-1, the nucleation temperature (T n) of Sample-3 is much higher. As a result, the undercooling (ΔT) of it is only 10.5°C, while it is 18.9°C for pure Al, and 13.4°C for Sample-1, indicating that a little ΔT is enough for the melt of Sample-3 to nucleate. The amount of TiB2 particles is equal to each other in the two cases of Sample-1 and Sample-3, therefore, the reason for the decrease of ΔT is mainly due to the change of the melt structure, namely, the formation of the Ti transition zone on the interface between TiB2 and aluminum melt in the state of liquid.

It should be noted that excess Ti also can result in the increase of Tn, and the increment can be calculated by the Al–Ti phase equilibrium. Because the ratio of Ti and B in TiB2 is 2.2/1, so the amount of the excess Ti added into the alloy is 0.0084% (0.3%×2.8%) for Sample-3. According to the Al–Ti phase equilibrium, the melting point increases nearly 4.63°C (665–660.37°C) when the amount of Ti increases from 0 to 0.15%. Therefore, the increment of T n caused by the excess Ti is 0.26°C (4.63°C×0.0084%/0.15%), which is much lower than the actual increment 2.8°C (650.7–647.9°C). This further proves that the reason for the increase of T n mainly lies in the change of the melt structure besides the addition of TiB2 particles. Here the change of melt structure is referred to the formation of the Ti transition zone on the interface between TiB2 and aluminum melt. Because of its formation, it is much easier for the alloy to nucleate, so it can nucleate with a smaller undercooling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relationship between viscosity and refining efficiency caused by Ti transition zone
ce:section.ce:para: Viscosity is defined as the force required to move a surface of unit area at unit velocity past an equivalent parallel surface at unit distance; it shows a measure of the capacity of a liquid to transmit a dynamic stress in shear. Many factors can affect the viscosity, such as temperature, the addition of external substance, etc. All the viscosity in this experiment was measured at the same temperature, so here two reasons might cause the increase of the viscosity: one is the addition of solid particles into the melt; the other is the change of the melt structure.

Samples 1–4 contain the same amount of TiB2 particles. The viscosity of Sample-1 only increases by 7.08%, however, that of Samples 2–4 increase by 16.51, 31.83 and 38.42%, respectively. Though there are also some TiAl3 compounds, most of the TiAl3 compounds will gradually decompose into Al and Ti atoms in the ambience of low Ti content [6]. This indicates that besides the addition of solid particles into the melt, the change of melt structure may be the main reason for the sudden increase of viscosity, which is considered to be the formation of the Ti transition zone on the interface between TiB2 and aluminum melt.

The Ti transition zone, meaning that Ti atoms distribute in gradient, is firstly formed on the interface between TiB2 and aluminum melt in the state of liquid, which can evolve into the Ti transition layer in the state of solid, and the schematic illustration of its formation model is presented in Fig. 7 . The Ti transition zone is not same to the TiAl3 layer around TiB2 particles found by Mohanty et al. [11,15]. In the present experiments, since no obvious substance was found around the TiB2 particle (Fig. 4), it is considered that it is not TiAl3 but a Ti-rich zone around the TiB2 particle. Ti atoms distribute around TiB2 particles in the concentration gradient, and it is possible that the Ti with higher content in the internal layer reacts with Al to form TiAl3 compound, and when the content of solute Ti is higher TiAl3 can be formed more easily. Therefore, TiAl3 layer around TiB2 is a particular case of the Ti transition zone.

Jones [16] has hypothesized the concept of “hypernucleation” at the TiB2/melt interface with no experiments proving it. By investigating the activities of Ti in the melt and in TiB2, he showed that Ti atoms will segregate to the TiB2/melt interface providing a stable layer of Ti and Al atoms on the TiB2 crystal surface, which will favor the growth of primary α-Al from the bulk with virtually no undercooling. However, though the present DSC experiment (shown in Fig. 6), it can be found that the nucleation of α-Al of Sample-3 is not without any undercooling as Jones said but with a small undercooling. Furthermore, the TEM analysis of the rapidly solidified ribbon shows the obvious Ti transition layer around TiB2 particles (Figs. 4 and 5), so it is considered that the Ti segregate to the TiB2/melt interface not just to form one layer of Ti and Al, but to form a bigger Ti-rich zone called as “Ti transition zone” and the Ti content is higher when it is nearer to the TiB2 particles. Because of the existance of Ti transition zone, the α-Al can easily nucleate and grow on it with a smaller driving power, so a little undercooling is enough for the nucleation.

The Ti transition zone has two properties contributory to nucleation: One lies in that it produces a thermally stable layer of pseudo-α-Al; the other is that the size match of Ti and Al facilitates the ordering of the Ti transition zone and adjacent melt layers, so that the Ti transition zone covering the inoculant particle disguises it as α-Al. In the state of casting, this pseudo-crystal of aluminum is an ideal prenucleus on which fresh α-Al grows further. Such a Ti transition zone even permits peritectic reaction occurring in the micro-zone and promotes the precipitation of the primary α-Al from the bulk melt to form around the TiB2 particles. Therefore, the formation of Ti transition zone is the main reason for the decrease of grain size.

At the same time, the formation of Ti transition zone is also the main reason for the sudden increase of melt viscosity. It is well known that the change of the melt structure is the main reason for the increase of viscosity after the addition of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. After its addition, a mass of TiB2 particles can be surrounded by Ti transition zone and because the size of the Ti transition zone is much bigger than that of TiB2 particles, so the melt becomes quite stickier than before, which results in the sudden increase of melt viscosity. Therefore, the relationship between the viscosity and the grain refinement efficiency is mainly caused by the formation of the Ti transition zone.

It is well understood that the TiAl3 and TiB2 are the main compounds besides the Al matrix in the Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. Because the surface of TiB2 particle is not slick but has many pits or steps [17], trace Ti atoms can stay in the pits or steps. But because the content of Ti is too high, nearly all Ti exists in the form of TiAl3. Furthermore, most TiB2 particles agglomerate, so only a few TiB2 particles have trace Ti atoms in their pits. After the addition of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy into the pure Al melt, the TiAl3 will gradually dissolve into Ti and Al atoms. For the TiB2 particles with trace Ti atoms in the pits, the dissociating Ti atoms in the melt can easily congregate to the TiB2 surface because of the existing of the trace Ti atoms, and form the Ti transition zone. There is no excess Ti in the Al–2.2Ti–1B master alloy, so no Ti atoms exist in the pits or steps of TiB2 particles. For this reason, it is not very easy for the dissociating Ti atoms in the melt to congregate to TiB2 surface even Al–2.2Ti–1B is added with Al–10Ti master alloy, so just few of Ti transition zone can be formed. Therefore, compared with Sample-3, the grain refining efficiency of Sample-2 is worse than it and the viscosity of Sample-2 also increase less than it.

While for the Al–5Ti–1B master alloy after strong distortion, TiB2 particles distribute more even, so more TiB2 particles have the chance to have trace Ti atoms in their pits or steps. Thus, more Ti transition zone around TiB2 particles can be formed after its addition, for which the grain refining efficiency of Sample-4 is better than Sample-3 and the increment of the viscosity of Sample-4 is also bigger than that of Sample-3.

Above all, the great decrease of grain refining efficiency and the sudden increase of viscosity are both caused by the change of melt structure, i.e. the formation of Ti transition zone. Therefore, the viscosity can be used to approximately estimate the refining efficiency of Al–Ti–B refiners in production to a certain extent.

ce:list-item.ce:para: When the grain size of the solidified samples is evener and finer, the viscosity of the melt is higher after the addition of different Al–Ti–B master alloys, indicating that viscosity can be used to approximately estimate the refining efficiency of Al–Ti–B refiners in production to a certain extent.

TEM analysis shows that there is a Ti transition layer on the interface between TiB2 and aluminum matrix in the rapidly solidified sample with the addition of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. The DSC experiment also shows that the T n increases markedly after the addition of a little amount of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy.

The change of melt structure, that is the formation of Ti transition zone on the interface between TiB2 and aluminum melt, is proved to be the main reason for the increase of the viscosity and the decrease of the grain size.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Shandong National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. Z2004F03) and the Key Project of Science and Technology Research of Ministry of Education of China (No. 106103).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metall. Trans. A (Phys. Metall. Mater. Sci.)
Proceedings of Sessions TMS Annual Meeting
